refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005 Refined: The NGHE solution shall automatically derive performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) from worksite mission context and operational parameters, record them in a traceable baseline, and maintain a managed history of all requirement updates and calibration changes with full audit trail.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on 'method' and lacked explicit traceability and history management. Refinement adds specificity: (1) automatic derivation from mission context, (2) explicit baseline recording, (3) managed history with audit trail. This aligns with acceptance criteria demanding 'documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs' and 'history of baselines/calibration updates.' High safety criticality demands explicit safety margin derivation and audit capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verification shall confirm: (a) performance targets are automatically computed from worksite mission inputs (e.g., terrain, payload, cycle count); (b) baseline is recorded with timestamp, operator ID, and mission context; (c) all updates are logged with before/after values and justification; (d) traceability chain from worksite input → derived requirement → baseline record is complete and auditable.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Derive performance targets from worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle time, energy budget, availability SLA).
CR-NGHE-005.2: Record derived performance targets as a baseline with full context (timestamp, operator, mission ID, environmental conditions).
CR-NGHE-005.3: Maintain a managed history of all performance requirement and calibration updates with audit trail (who, when, why, before/after values).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Ensure traceability from worksite input → derived requirement → baseline record → operational decision.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized performance derivation engine (rules-based or ML-based) that ingests worksite mission context and outputs performance targets; coupled with a versioned baseline repository and audit-logged update mechanism.
DP-B: Distributed derivation logic embedded in Vehicle and Teleoperation modules, with a central registry that collects and reconciles derived targets; audit trail maintained per module.
DP-C: Manual performance requirement entry by Site Manager with system-assisted validation against mission context; all entries logged with operator context and timestamp.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Centralized performance derivation engine with versioned baseline repository and audit-logged update mechanism. The engine ingests worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle count, safety plan constraints) and applies deterministic or ML-based rules to derive performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins). All derived baselines are recorded with full context (timestamp, mission ID, operator, environmental conditions). All updates are audit-logged with before/after values and justification. Traceability is maintained via unique baseline IDs linked to mission context.

potential_sources: Coupling to Telemetry interface: derivation engine must ingest real-time or historical telemetry (vehicle state, energy consumption, cycle times) to validate and refine targets. This is a logical dependency, not a design flaw.
Coupling to Tasking interface: mission context (payload, terrain, cycle count) flows from Tasking into the derivation engine. This is expected and decoupled via interface contract.
Coupling to SafetyAlert: safety plan constraints and incident history inform safety margin derivation. This is a logical dependency, not a design flaw.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A exhibits expected functional coupling (information flow) but not structural coupling. The derivation engine is logically independent; it consumes inputs via well-defined interfaces and produces outputs (baseline records) that are consumed by other modules. No circular dependencies detected. Centralization reduces diffusion risk compared to DP-B.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context is available in structured form (e.g., JSON, database record) at the time of requirement derivation. RISK: If mission context is incomplete or unstructured, derivation may fail or produce invalid targets. MITIGATION: Define mission context schema and validation rules.

ASSUMPTION: Performance target derivation rules are stable and well-understood (e.g., cycle time = f(terrain, payload, vehicle capability)). RISK: If rules are poorly calibrated or change frequently, baseline targets may be unreliable. MITIGATION: Establish rule validation process and version control.

ASSUMPTION: Audit trail storage and query performance are acceptable for high-frequency updates (e.g., multiple baselines per shift). RISK: Audit log bloat may degrade query performance. MITIGATION: Implement log rotation and archival strategy.

ASSUMPTION: Safety criticality (high) requires deterministic, explainable derivation logic. RISK: ML-based derivation may be opaque and difficult to certify. MITIGATION: Use rule-based derivation for safety-critical targets; ML only for non-critical optimization targets.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly separate safety-critical target derivation (deterministic, rule-based, auditable) from optimization targets (may use ML). Define the derivation rule set as a controlled artifact with version control and change approval process. Specify audit trail schema (who, when, what, why) and retention policy. Clarify the interface contract for mission context input (schema, validation rules, error handling).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary. It contains all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and cross-cutting services (performance derivation, baseline management, audit logging). The performance derivation engine, baseline repository, and audit trail are realized as services within the NGHE Solution, accessible to all subsystems via well-defined interfaces.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound telemetry from Vehicle (energy consumption, cycle times, operational state) used to validate and refine performance targets. Also historical telemetry for trend analysis and baseline calibration.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Inbound mission context (payload, terrain, cycle count, safety plan reference, operator ID) from Site Manager or Tele-operator. Triggers performance requirement derivation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Inbound safety plan constraints and incident history from Safety Services. Outbound derived safety margins and performance targets to Safety Services for validation and enforcement.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Outbound baseline records and audit trail to external systems (e.g., site management system, compliance database). Inbound queries for baseline history and traceability.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level boundary. Performance derivation, baseline management, and audit logging are cross-cutting services that logically belong at the system level. All subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) depend on these services. Containment is high because the derivation engine is a single, centralized artifact. Diffusion risk is minimal (5%) because the engine is not replicated across subsystems.

Vehicle module is responsible for physical operation and telemetry generation. Performance derivation logic does not belong in Vehicle; it is a system-level concern. If derivation logic is embedded in Vehicle, it will be duplicated or inconsistently applied across different vehicle types. Diffusion risk is high (80%) because Vehicle is a hardware-centric module with limited software extensibility.

Teleoperation module is responsible for remote operation and tele-operator interface. Performance derivation is not a teleoperation concern. If derivation logic is embedded in Teleoperation, it will be tightly coupled to tele-operator workflow and difficult to reuse for autonomous or site-manager-initiated derivation. Diffusion risk is high (85%).

Safety Services is responsible for safety plan enforcement and incident management. Safety margin derivation is a safety concern, but general performance target derivation (cycle time, energy efficiency) is not. If all derivation logic is embedded in Safety Services, non-safety targets will be orphaned or duplicated elsewhere. Partial containment (40%) is possible if Safety Services owns only safety-critical target derivation; general targets should remain in NGHE Solution. Diffusion risk is moderate-high (60%).

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: NGHE Solution (system-level service). Performance derivation engine, baseline repository, and audit trail are cross-cutting services that must be owned at the system level to ensure consistency, traceability, and reusability across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The performance derivation engine is a single, centralized artifact that ingests mission context and produces baseline records. It is not replicated or distributed across subsystems. All subsystems access the engine via well-defined interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link). Containment is high (95%) because the engine is logically and physically localized in the NGHE Solution module. Diffusion risk is minimal because there is a single source of truth for performance targets.
containment_risks: RISK: If the derivation engine is implemented as a monolithic service, it may become a bottleneck or single point of failure. MITIGATION: Design the engine with redundancy and failover capability. Consider microservice architecture if scalability is a concern.

RISK: If the baseline repository is not properly indexed or partitioned, query performance may degrade with large audit logs. MITIGATION: Implement efficient indexing (e.g., by mission ID, timestamp, operator) and log rotation/archival strategy.

RISK: If the derivation rules are not version-controlled or change-managed, baseline reproducibility may be compromised. MITIGATION: Establish a controlled artifact process for rule updates with approval and audit trail.

RISK: If the interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are not clearly defined, subsystems may make inconsistent assumptions about derivation behavior. MITIGATION: Document interface contracts with examples and error handling.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the internal architecture of the NGHE Solution module: (1) Define the performance derivation engine as a service with a well-defined API (inputs: mission context, telemetry; outputs: baseline records). (2) Define the baseline repository as a versioned, audited data store with query and update APIs. (3) Define the audit trail as a separate, append-only log with retention policy. (4) Specify the interface contracts for Tasking (mission context schema), Telemetry (telemetry data schema), SafetyAlert (safety constraint schema), and Network Link (baseline query/export schema). (5) Consider microservice or containerized deployment to enable independent scaling and failover.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR clearly specifies what the system must do: derive, record, and manage performance requirements from worksite mission context with full traceability and audit trail. DP-A (centralized derivation engine with versioned baseline repository and audit logging) directly addresses all FR elements: (1) derivation from mission context, (2) recording with baseline context, (3) managed history with audit trail, (4) traceability. The DP is logically sound and decoupled from subsystem-specific concerns. No circular dependencies or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK (LOW): If the derivation engine is implemented as a monolithic service without clear module boundaries, future changes to derivation rules may require changes to multiple subsystems. MITIGATION: Define the engine as a service with a stable API; subsystems depend on the API, not the implementation.

DIFFUSION RISK (LOW): If the baseline repository is not properly abstracted, subsystems may directly query the repository, creating tight coupling. MITIGATION: Provide a query API that abstracts the repository schema; subsystems use the API, not direct database access.

DIFFUSION RISK (MODERATE): If audit logging is implemented inconsistently across subsystems, audit trail completeness may be compromised. MITIGATION: Provide a centralized audit logging service that all subsystems use; enforce audit logging at the API level.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate boundary for the performance derivation engine, baseline repository, and audit trail. Containment score is high (95%) because these are cross-cutting services that must be centralized to ensure consistency and traceability. The engine is not replicated across subsystems; all subsystems access it via well-defined interfaces. Ownership is clear: NGHE Solution owns the engine and repository; subsystems own the interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) that feed into the engine.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible. The refined FR specifies observable, testable requirements: (1) performance targets are derived from mission context (testable via test cases with known mission inputs and expected outputs), (2) baseline is recorded with full context (testable via database inspection), (3) audit trail is maintained (testable via audit log inspection), (4) traceability is complete (testable via traceability matrix). The IM assignment (NGHE Solution) provides a clear boundary for verification: all derivation, recording, and audit logic is contained within the NGHE Solution module and can be tested independently.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear. The refined FR is allocated to the NGHE Solution module, which owns the performance derivation engine, baseline repository, and audit trail. Sub-FRs are allocated as follows: CR-NGHE-005.1 (derive targets) → derivation engine; CR-NGHE-005.2 (record baseline) → baseline repository; CR-NGHE-005.3 (maintain history) → audit trail; CR-NGHE-005.4 (ensure traceability) → traceability service (part of NGHE Solution). External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clearly identified and allocated to subsystems that provide/consume them.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1: Clarify the derivation rule set. Define which performance targets are safety-critical (deterministic, rule-based) and which are optimization targets (may use ML). Establish a controlled artifact process for rule updates with version control and change approval.

REVISION 2: Define the mission context schema. Specify the structure, validation rules, and error handling for mission context inputs (payload, terrain, cycle count, safety plan reference, operator ID). Provide examples and test cases.
REVISION 3: Define the audit trail schema and retention policy. Specify the fields (who, when, what, why, before/after values), storage format, query API, and log rotation/archival strategy.

REVISION 4: Specify the interface contracts for Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link. Define the data schemas, error handling, and expected behavior for each interface. Provide examples and test cases.

REVISION 5: Consider the deployment architecture. Should the derivation engine be a monolithic service, a microservice, or a containerized component? What are the scalability, failover, and performance requirements?

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-structured and decomposable into four sub-FRs (derive targets, record baseline, maintain history, ensure traceability). The selected DP (centralized derivation engine with versioned baseline repository and audit logging) is logically sound and addresses all FR elements. The assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is appropriate and has high containment (95%) with minimal diffusion risk. All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass. However, the sub-FRs are not yet atomic and require further decomposition to reach implementation-ready specifications. Specifically, CR-NGHE-005.1 (derive targets) requires detailed specification of derivation rules and algorithms; CR-NGHE-005.2 (record baseline) requires specification of baseline data structure and storage; CR-NGHE-005.3 (maintain history) requires specification of audit trail schema and retention policy; CR-NGHE-005.4 (ensure traceability) requires specification of traceability mechanisms and identifiers. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify these details and enable detailed design and implementation.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1.1: Define and implement performance target derivation rules for cycle time based on terrain, payload, and vehicle capability.
CR-NGHE-005.1.2: Define and implement performance target derivation rules for energy efficiency based on mission profile and vehicle efficiency characteristics.
CR-NGHE-005.1.3: Define and implement performance target derivation rules for availability based on maintenance schedule and historical reliability data.
CR-NGHE-005.1.4: Define and implement performance target derivation rules for safety margins based on safety plan constraints and incident history.
CR-NGHE-005.2.1: Define baseline data structure (fields, types, constraints) and storage schema.
CR-NGHE-005.2.2: Implement baseline recording API with validation and error handling.
CR-NGHE-005.3.1: Define audit trail schema (who, when, what, why, before/after values) and storage format.
CR-NGHE-005.3.2: Implement audit logging mechanism with append-only semantics and query API.
CR-NGHE-005.3.3: Define audit log retention policy and archival strategy.
CR-NGHE-005.4.1: Define traceability identifiers (baseline ID, mission ID, requirement ID) and linkage mechanisms.
CR-NGHE-005.4.2: Implement traceability query API to retrieve the chain from worksite input → derived requirement → baseline record → operational decision.

assumptions: Worksite mission context is available in structured form (JSON, database record) at the time of requirement derivation.
Performance target derivation rules are stable and well-understood (e.g., cycle time = f(terrain, payload, vehicle capability)).
Audit trail storage and query performance are acceptable for high-frequency updates (multiple baselines per shift).
Safety criticality (high) requires deterministic, explainable derivation logic; ML-based derivation is not suitable for safety-critical targets.
The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate boundary for cross-cutting services (derivation engine, baseline repository, audit trail).
All subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) access the derivation engine via well-defined interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link).
The derivation engine is a single, centralized artifact that is not replicated across subsystems.
open_risks: RISK: Derivation rule calibration. If rules are poorly calibrated, baseline targets may be unreliable. MITIGATION: Establish rule validation process and version control.
RISK: Audit log bloat. If audit logs grow too large, query performance may degrade. MITIGATION: Implement log rotation and archival strategy.

RISK: Interface contract clarity. If interface contracts are not clearly defined, subsystems may make inconsistent assumptions. MITIGATION: Document interface contracts with examples and error handling.
RISK: Deployment architecture. If the derivation engine is a monolithic service, it may become a bottleneck. MITIGATION: Consider microservice or containerized deployment.

RISK: Safety certification. If derivation logic is complex or uses ML, it may be difficult to certify for safety-critical applications. MITIGATION: Use rule-based derivation for safety-critical targets; establish certification process.
evidence_needed: Worksite mission context schema and examples (payload, terrain, cycle count, safety plan reference, operator ID).
Performance target derivation rules and algorithms (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) with validation data.
Baseline data structure and storage schema with examples.
Audit trail schema and retention policy with examples.
Interface contracts for Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link with data schemas and examples.
Deployment architecture and scalability analysis for the derivation engine.
Safety certification plan for safety-critical derivation rules.
Test cases and acceptance criteria for each sub-FR.
